structuredFacts.title: Kubernetes
structuredFacts.subtitle: Open-source container software
structuredFacts.leadParagraph:
Kubernetes (), also known as K8s, is an open-source container orchestration system for automating software deployment, scaling, and management. Originally designed by Google, the project is now maintained by a worldwide community of contributors, and the trademark is held by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation.
The name Kubernetes comes from the Ancient Greek term κυβερνήτης, kubernḗtēs (helmsman, pilot), which is also the origin of the words cybernetics and (through Latin) governor. "Kubernetes" is often abbreviated with the numerical contraction "K8s", meaning "the letter K, followed by 8 letters, followed by s".
Kubernetes assembles one or more computers, either virtual machines or bare metal, into a cluster which can run workloads in containers. It works with various container runtimes, such as containerd and CRI-O. Its suitability for running and managing workloads of all sizes and styles has led to its widespread adoption in clouds and data centers. There are multiple distributions of this platform—from independent software vendors (ISVs) as well as hosted-on-cloud offerings from all the major public cloud vendors.
The software consists of a control plane and nodes on which the actual applications run. It includes tools like kubeadm and kubectl which can be used to interact with its REST-based API.
structuredFacts.briefSummary: Originating from the Ancient Greek word for helmsman or pilot, kubernētēs is the etymological root of both cybernetics and governor. Today, developers frequently refer to the technology as K8s, a numeronym where the number eight represents the letters between the starting K and the ending s. Google originally designed the technology and officially announced it in June 2014. Yet, while Google's internal Borg system was written in C++, engineers chose to write Kubernetes in Go. This choice proved successful; by March 2018, the project ranked second only to the Linux kernel on GitHub for active authors and issues.

cards.title: The Helmsman of Modern Software
cards.summary: Ancient Greek sailors originally used the word kubernētēs to describe a ship's helmsman or pilot. Centuries later, Google engineers sought a name that would reflect their project's purpose of steering distributed software systems. This same linguistic root also birthed the modern English words cybernetics and governor. Consequently, they chose Kubernetes, establishing both a nautical theme and a shorthand numeronym. Today, developers globally refer to this dominant cloud technology using the contraction K8s. Ultimately, ancient Greek navigation terminology inspired modern cloud container orchestration.
cards.readerQuestion: What inspired the creation of a new system to steer modern software containers?
cards.keyTakeaway: Ancient Greek navigation terms inspired modern cloud container orchestration.

cards.title: From Borg to Go
cards.summary: Google engineers originally codenamed their new container project 'Seven of Nine' in a nod to the famous Star Trek character. Their goal was to modernize and externalize their proprietary cluster management system for the public cloud era. Today, the seven spokes on the official Kubernetes logo wheel subtly honor that original codename. This design choice created an open-source platform built on Go that still respects its internal heritage. Unlike the original C++ Borg system, developers completely rebuilt this new platform using the Go programming language. Ultimately, Google successfully transformed its private C++ infrastructure into a modern, open-source powerhouse.
cards.readerQuestion: How did Google engineers transform their internal infrastructure into an open-source project?
cards.keyTakeaway: Google rebuilt its internal C++ infrastructure into open-source Go.

cards.title: The Open Cloud Alliance
cards.summary: Google officially announced the Kubernetes project in June 2014. The search giant sought to seed an open-source standard to challenge proprietary cloud lock-in. In July 2015, Google partnered with the Linux Foundation to form the Cloud Native Computing Foundation. Consequently, major cloud competitors abandoned their proprietary alternatives to natively support the platform. Developers launched Kubernetes Version 1.0 on July 21, 2015. This open-source collaboration united fierce competitors behind a single orchestration standard.
cards.readerQuestion: How did Kubernetes achieve industry-wide backing and establish its core orchestration capabilities?
cards.keyTakeaway: Open-source collaboration united fierce competitors behind one standard.

cards.title: The Rise of Kubernetes
cards.summary: Google launched Kubernetes as an open-source container orchestrator written in Go. Seeking to share the power of their internal Borg system with the public, engineers designed a tool that quickly captured the industry's attention. By March 2018, the project ranked second only to Linux in active authors. This rapid rise established the platform as a dominant force in global cloud infrastructure. On March 6, 2018, Kubernetes climbed to ninth place in total commits on GitHub. The technology had officially become the world's second largest open-source project.
cards.readerQuestion: How massive did the Kubernetes ecosystem grow compared to other open-source giants?
cards.keyTakeaway: Kubernetes became the world's second largest open-source project.

cards.title: The Core of Consensus
cards.summary: The etcd data store holds the entire state of a Kubernetes cluster. To maintain a single source of truth across multiple nodes, distributed systems require a reliable way to manage cluster state. According to the CAP theorem, etcd intentionally prioritizes consistency over availability when network partitions occur. By sacrificing write availability during these partitions, the system guarantees strict state consistency and prevents split-brain scenarios. It achieves this by rejecting writes on the minority side of a network split, thereby preventing data divergence. Ultimately, etcd ensures that strict consistency always takes precedence over availability during network partitions.
cards.readerQuestion: How does the underlying data store maintain order across distributed networks?
cards.keyTakeaway: Etcd sacrifices availability to guarantee absolute data consistency.

timeline.headline: Kubernetes Announced by Google
timeline.description: Google officially announced the Kubernetes project, conceived by employees Joe Beda, Brendan Burns, and Craig McLuckie.

timeline.headline: Kubernetes Version 1.0 Released
timeline.description: The first production-ready version of Kubernetes was released, establishing its core container orchestration capabilities.

timeline.headline: CNCF Formed with Kubernetes
timeline.description: Google partnered with the Linux Foundation to form the Cloud Native Computing Foundation, donating Kubernetes as its seed technology.

timeline.headline: Competitors Announce Native Support
timeline.description: Major industry competitors including VMware, Mesosphere, Docker, Microsoft Azure, and AWS announced native integration for Kubernetes.

timeline.headline: GitHub Ranking Milestone Reached
timeline.description: Kubernetes reached ninth place in commits and second place in authors and issues on GitHub, trailing only the Linux kernel.

timeline.headline: N-3 Support Policy Adopted
timeline.description: Starting with version 1.19, Kubernetes transitioned from an N-2 to an N-3 support policy for minor versions.
